questions.question: Why did 3D Gaussian Splatting largely replace NeRF as the production default for photorealistic scene reconstruction by 2026?
options: Gaussian Splatting produces higher image quality than NeRF in every case

3DGS is explicit (no MLP per pixel): rendering is GPU rasterisation at 100+ fps, training takes minutes instead of hours, scenes are directly editable, and Khronos + OpenUSD both standardised it in 2026
NeRF was retracted from the research record
PyTorch stopped supporting NeRF operators
questions.explanation: 3DGS swaps an implicit MLP for millions of explicit 3D Gaussians. Rendering becomes sorted alpha compositing that GPUs run at 100+ fps on consumer hardware. Training runs in minutes. Every Gaussian is directly editable (move the chair by translating its Gaussians). By 2026 Khronos ratified a glTF extension and OpenUSD 26.03 shipped a native schema, making 3DGS a portable production format.

questions.question: Each 3D Gaussian stores colour as spherical harmonics (SH) coefficients rather than a fixed RGB triple. What advantage does this provide over fixed RGB?
options: It reduces total memory below what plain RGB would require
It prevents gradient explosion during rasterisation
It is required by the glTF KHR_gaussian_splatting export format
It encodes how colour varies with viewing direction, capturing specular highlights, view-dependent sheen, and reflections without per-view textures or an MLP
questions.explanation: Spherical harmonics are the Fourier basis on the unit sphere. Each Gaussian stores learned SH coefficients per RGB channel (degree 3 = 16 coefficients = 48 floats). At render time, `eval_sh_degree_3(sh_coeffs, view_dir)` evaluates them against the current viewing direction to produce a 3-vector RGB. This captures Lambertian shading, mild specular, and subtle reflections that a fixed RGB colour cannot represent.

questions.question: The 3D Gaussian covariance `Sigma` projects to a 2D screen-space covariance via `Sigma' = J W Sigma W^T J^T`. What is J in this formula?
options: The Jacobian of the perspective projection at the Gaussian's world position; it linearises the nonlinear perspective map so the 3D ellipsoid transforms correctly into a 2D ellipse on screen
The camera's intrinsic matrix K (focal length and principal point)
A randomly initialised weight matrix that is learned during training
The camera rotation matrix (same as W)
questions.explanation: Perspective projection is nonlinear, so a 3D Gaussian does not project exactly to a 2D Gaussian. The Jacobian J linearises the projection at the Gaussian's position mu, giving the first-order approximation that maps the 3D covariance ellipsoid to a 2D screen-space covariance ellipse. This is what makes every pixel inside the ellipse receive the correct Gaussian contribution weighted by exp(-0.5 * d^T Sigma'^{-1} d).

questions.question: In `eval_2d_gaussian(means, covs, points)`, the einsum `d = torch.einsum('gpi,gij,gpj->gp', diff, inv, diff)` computes which quantity?
options: The L1 distance between each Gaussian centre and each pixel position
The cosine similarity between Gaussian colour and pixel RGB
The quadratic form `diff^T Sigma^{-1} diff` — the squared Mahalanobis distance from every pixel to every Gaussian centre, used in the Gaussian density exponent
A normalisation constant so all Gaussian densities sum to 1
questions.explanation: The einsum contracts over the i (x/y) and j (x/y) indices to compute diff^T Sigma^{-1} diff for every (Gaussian g, pixel p) pair simultaneously. This is the Mahalanobis distance, which accounts for the Gaussian's anisotropic shape: a flat elongated Gaussian correctly gives high density along its major axis. The result feeds into `exp(-0.5 * d)` to produce the per-(Gaussian, pixel) density used by `rasterise_2d`.

questions.question: During 3DGS training, densification includes 'clone' and 'split' operations. What triggers each, and what does 'prune' remove?
options: Nothing — Gaussians are fixed at initialisation and only their parameters are updated

High gradient magnitude with small scale triggers clone (more local detail needed); high gradient with large scale triggers split (one Gaussian too coarse to fit the region); opacity-below-threshold triggers prune
Clone and split happen at fixed iteration counts; prune is manual
All three are triggered randomly to explore parameter space
questions.explanation: Adaptive densification is what lets 3DGS grow from ~100k SfM-seeded Gaussians to 1-5M at convergence. Clone duplicates under-resolved small Gaussians to add local detail; split breaks a single over-large Gaussian into two smaller ones when it is too smooth to fit its region; prune drops Gaussians whose sigmoid opacity has decayed below the threshold. These three operations plus gradient descent on (mu, q, s, alpha, c_lm) are the complete training dynamics.

questions.question: The per-pixel colour equation `C = sum_i alpha_i * T_i * c_i` (with `T_i = prod_{j<i}(1 - alpha_j)`) appears in both NeRF and 3DGS. What does this shared equation reveal about the two methods?
options: NeRF and 3DGS are mathematically identical in every respect, including how they store the scene
NeRF is strictly higher quality because it uses an MLP
3DGS is strictly higher quality because rasterisation is faster

Both integrate the same volumetric rendering equation; they differ only in representation (implicit MLP vs explicit sparse Gaussians) and rendering procedure (ray marching vs rasterisation) — which is why their image quality is comparable despite the speed difference
questions.explanation: The shared equation is the classical volumetric render. NeRF evaluates it by sampling along rays and querying an MLP at each point. 3DGS evaluates it by projecting Gaussians to 2D and alpha-compositing sorted primitives per pixel. Same physics, same loss, different data structures and rendering algorithms. That identity explains why both achieve similar final PSNR/LPIPS; the practical gap is in speed (100+ fps vs seconds per frame) and editability.
